skill.name: python-executor
skill.description: Run Python scripts through the inference.sh sandbox with common data, media, web, and automation libraries preinstalled.
skill.summary: Execute short Python tasks in a hosted sandbox with file outputs and common scientific, media, and web libraries.
skill.author: halt-catch-fire

security_audit.summary: Most command findings are false positives caused by markdown backticks and code fences. The real risk is that the skill runs user-provided Python through a third-party CLI and demonstrates outbound network access from that sandbox.

remediation.issue: Remote execution may expose sensitive user data.
remediation.suggestion: Warn users not to include secrets, private files, credentials, or regulated data in submitted Python code or inputs.

remediation.issue: Outbound network access is demonstrated in examples.
remediation.suggestion: Document network boundaries, allowed destinations, logging behavior, and any provider-side restrictions for web requests.

remediation.issue: Installation depends on a third-party CLI.
remediation.suggestion: Link to trust, authentication, and uninstall guidance for belt before asking users to log in or run jobs.

static_findings.snippet: > **Install the belt CLI skill:** `npx skills add belt-sh/cli`
Files saved to `outputs/` are automatically returned:
description: "Execute Python code in a safe sandboxed environment via [inference.sh](https://inferen

semantic_findings.title: Third-Party Remote Python Execution
semantic_findings.description: The skill sends user-provided Python code to inference.sh through the belt CLI. This can expose code, data, prompts, or secrets included in the submitted job to a third-party service.
semantic_findings.confidence_reasoning: The metadata grants Bash access for belt, and the quick start submits arbitrary code through belt app run. The risk is inherent to remote code execution services.

high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The metadata grants Bash access for belt, and the quick start submits arbitrary code through belt app run. The risk is inherent to remote code execution services.

content.user_title: Run Python Code in a Hosted Sandbox
content.value_statement: Many agents need Python execution without local environment setup. This skill runs Python through inference.sh with common libraries and returns generated outputs.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python code through the infsh/python-executor app using the belt CLI.
Supports data analysis with NumPy, Pandas, SciPy, Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly.
Creates output files such as charts, CSV files, images, videos, PDFs, and 3D models.
Demonstrates HTTP requests, HTML parsing, browser automation, and API calls from the sandbox.
Supports configurable timeouts from 1 to 300 seconds and a high-memory variant.
Uses a CPU-only Python 3.10 environment with common media and document libraries.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and an inference.sh account before use.
Runs in a third-party hosted environment, so submitted code and data leave the local machine.
Does not provide GPU acceleration or dedicated machine learning runtimes.
Interactive displays are not supported; files must be saved to outputs for return.

use_cases.title: Analyze Small Datasets
use_cases.description: Process tabular data, calculate summaries, and create charts without configuring a local Python environment.

use_cases.title: Generate Media Assets
use_cases.description: Create images, short videos, PDFs, and simple 3D files from Python scripts and return the saved outputs.

use_cases.title: Test Automation Snippets
use_cases.description: Run short Python automation scripts, API checks, and parsing tasks in a disposable hosted runtime.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Simple Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to run a short Python script that prints a greeting and basic runtime information.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a Dataset
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to load my CSV data, calculate summary statistics, and save a chart to the outputs folder.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Visual Asset
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to generate an image or PDF from my specifications and return the saved output file.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Network-Aware Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to fetch public data from an approved URL, parse the response, and save a clean result file.

output_examples.input: Analyze a small sales table and create a bar chart.
output_examples.output: Returns a concise data summary and a saved chart file in the outputs folder.
Returns a generated image file and a short confirmation message.

output_examples.input: Fetch public profile data from an approved API endpoint.
output_examples.output: Returns formatted public fields and notes the external request that was made.

best_practices: Submit only the minimum code and data needed for the task.
Save every required artifact to the outputs folder before the script ends.
Set timeouts that match the expected workload and keep scripts non-interactive.
anti_patterns: Do not submit secrets, private credentials, or regulated data to the hosted executor.
Do not use the sandbox for unauthorized scraping, scanning, or policy-violating automation.
Do not expect local files, local credentials, GPUs, or interactive windows to be available.

faq.question: Does this run Python locally?
faq.answer: No. It submits Python jobs to the inference.sh hosted executor through the belt CLI.

faq.question: What tools must be installed?
faq.answer: The skill requires the belt CLI and access to the inference.sh service.

faq.question: Can it create files?
faq.answer: Yes. Files saved to the outputs folder are returned by the executor.

faq.question: Can it access the internet?
faq.answer: The examples show outbound HTTP requests from the sandbox. Users should confirm allowed use before making network calls.

faq.question: Is it safe for secrets?
faq.answer: No. Do not submit credentials, private files, tokens, or sensitive personal data to a third-party execution service.

faq.question: Does it support GPU workloads?
faq.answer: No. The documented environment is CPU-only and is not intended for dedicated machine learning workloads.
